rt1-8-1.title: Hands-on: expose into the 2.0-3.0 density window
rt1-8-1.intro: Film contrast lives on the characteristic curve, so where your density lands decides what you can see. Set the exposure time on an Ir-192 shot to put the film in the 2.0-3.0 target window - past the lazy toe - then prove that density follows total exposure E = A x t by compensating after the source decays.

tasks.label: Land the film density inside the 2.0-3.0 window
tasks.hint: Density climbs with total exposure E = A x t - lengthen the time slider to climb the characteristic curve past the low-gradient toe below D 2.0.

tasks.label: After 30 days of decay, restore density with time
tasks.hint: Ir-192's half-life is 73.8 days, so 30 days cuts activity to about 75% - stretch the exposure time roughly a third to keep E = A x t, and the density, the same.

rt1-8-2.title: Hands-on: tame Ug and place the IQI source side
rt1-8-2.intro: Definition dies by geometry: this setup starts with a smeared penumbra. Shrink Ug = F x t / d below the 0.5 mm code cap by pressing the film against the part and backing the source up, then drag the wire IQI onto the source-side weld surface - the position where image quality is hardest to prove.

tasks.label: Bring geometric unsharpness under 0.5 mm
tasks.hint: Ug = F x t / d: drag the object-to-film gap toward zero AND back the source up - doubling the stand-off halves the penumbra.

tasks.label: Drag the wire IQI onto the source-side weld face
tasks.hint: Source side is the default because that is where quality is hardest to demonstrate - film-side placement is rejected here (in the field it needs a lead 'F').
